strengths: Placeholder integrity is excellent — all seven template-literal placeholders (including complex expressions like ${SNAPSHOT_MAX_ENTRIES.toLocaleString()} and ${rejectedAbc + rejectedObs}) appear verbatim in Lao. Brand/acronym preservation is also strong: BehaviorLens, AlloFlow, Gemini, ABA, ABC, IEP, BIP, FBA, BCBA, BACB, FERPA, MTSS, RTI, DTT, DRA/DRI/DRO/DRL, SCD, FCT all appear unaltered, and the AI consent FERPA note even includes a culturally-localized codename example ('Brave Otter' → 'ນາກກ້າຫານ').

When the source did not contain an em-dash, the Lao text is generally fluent and natural-sounding: subtitles, hub tile descriptions, ABC labels, the AI-on/AI-off toggle text, the FERPA reminder, the confirm-dialog set, the parent/coping/reinforcer label sets, and the cycle phases all read as native Lao. Script is consistent: 100% Lao script throughout the translated portion, no Thai/Khmer leakage.
Educator-to-family register is maintained where translation happened — formal but warm, second-person ທ່ານ used consistently, no slangy or overly academic tone shifts.
risks: Roughly 350+ behavior_lens strings (about 15%) are still entirely in English because of a systematic pre-processing failure: any source string containing an em-dash (—) was either (a) left wholly untranslated with the dash replaced by a comma, or (b) had the dash replaced by a colon and surrounding English retained. Many of these are user-facing toasts, hub-tile descriptions, the Family Voice textarea placeholder, the live-session dropdown ': Choose a student:', and the critical 'Score (1,5):' which a Lao reader can misread as the decimal 1.5. A native Lao speaker should NOT be the first person asked to fix these — a regex re-run against the canonical English with em-dashes left intact is the right remediation.

Several ABA jargon translations are lexically off in ways a Lao educator might or might not catch but a Lao-speaking BCBA almost certainly would: 'Fidelity Checklist' → 'honesty checklist'; 'Reinforcement Inventory' → 'reinforcement goods-warehouse'; 'verify all claims' in the AI disclaimer → 'verify all rights'; 'Acting-Out Cycle' → 'expression cycle'; 'Caseload Dashboard' → opaque coinage. Recommend a Lao SLP/special educator scan all hub_*_title strings and the ai.print_disclaimer specifically.

Vocabulary is inconsistent across keys (ຜົນສະທ້ອນ vs ຜົນຕາມມາ for 'consequence'; ການເພີ່ມລະດັບ vs ການເລັ່ງຕົວ for 'acceleration'). Without a glossary pass, the same English ABA concept appears under different Lao terms across tools, which will confuse pilot teachers reading multiple hub tiles.

strengths: Template placeholders (${label}, ${count}, ${when}, ${SNAPSHOT_MAX_ENTRIES.toLocaleString()}, ${rejectedAbc + rejectedObs}) are preserved verbatim in every sampled instance — no critical placeholder loss.
Brand names and clinical acronyms (BehaviorLens, AlloFlow, Gemini, ABA, BIP, FBA, IEP, BCBA, FERPA, MTSS, RTI, SCD, GAS, DTT, FCT, MI, JSON, SVG) are kept intact and not erroneously translated.
Script is consistently Latin throughout, appropriate for Acholi (Lwo); diacritics and special characters not required and not misused.
risks: High volume of English-leakage strings — long passages alternate Acholi and untranslated English (e.g., 'Module loading', 'If this persists, check', 'Record antecedent, behavior, ki consequence observations'). A native speaker should re-translate at least the *_desc keys in hub.* end-to-end.

The word 'Matter' is used as a calque for 'Data' across dozens of keys (abc_title, matter_collection, matter_sources, matter sheet, etc.). 'Matter' is not the English word for data and is not Acholi — this looks like a machine-translation artifact and risks confusing teachers. Should be replaced with a consistent Acholi term for 'data' (e.g., 'gin acoya' or similar) or kept verbatim as 'Data'.

Proper nouns and locale-specific UI labels are inconsistently capitalized ('latin kwan', 'kilas', 'gang' all lowercased mid-sentence and in titles). A native reviewer should set a capitalization convention; current state looks unfinished.

strengths: Confirm/dialog block (behavior_lens.confirm.*) and the AI consent panel (behavior_lens.ai.*) are genuinely fluent Somali with placeholders ${count}/${label}/${when}/${SNAPSHOT_MAX_ENTRIES.toLocaleString()} preserved verbatim and brand tokens (BehaviorLens, Gemini, FERPA) kept intact — proves the translator had the skill when they slowed down.
Acronym/brand preservation is broadly correct across the pack: ABA, ABC, IEP, BIP, FBA, BCBA, FERPA, MTSS, RTI, IOA, DTT, DR, GAS, SCD, FCT, JSON, CSV all retained unchanged.
When short single-word labels were translated they were done well (Qoraallo, Eegisyada, Sax!, La koobiyeeyay!, tus. for e.g.) and emoji glyphs were preserved throughout.
risks: This is not actually Maay Maay (Af-Maay) — it is Somali orthography ('iyo', 'kaaga', 'wuu kara', 'ku') with no Af-Maay-specific phonology/markers visible. A Maay-speaking refugee family from southern Somalia will read it as approximate Somali at best, not their home language. A true Maay native speaker MUST audit before pilot.

Massive Spanglish/code-mixing in behavior_lens.hub.*_desc and behavior_lens.ph.* and most descriptive sentences: the translator (or upstream tool) appears to have used a brittle word-substitution pass that replaced only common function words (and→iyo, with→la, to→ku, your→kaaga, what→maxay, time→waqti) while leaving every content noun/verb in English. ~60-70% of mid-length UI sentences are effectively still English; this pack should NOT ship to families in its current state.

Specific catastrophic strings to fix before any deploy: 'wuu kara't Do' (cantdowontdo_title — corrupted from Can't), 'Saaxir Behavior Lens' ('Wizard'→'sorcerer' wrong register + brand split), 'wuu kara'='he can' used for generic 'you can' (gender error), 'Naftiisa-Check' ('his self' for student-facing label), 'Aan jecelahay' ('I love' as a noun label for Favorites), 'Wo dhacay' (non-standard particle), and 'Shift+Geli' (mis-translated keyboard chord — physical key is labeled Enter).

strengths: Template-literal placeholders (${count}, ${label}, ${when}, ${SNAPSHOT_MAX_ENTRIES.toLocaleString()}, ${rejectedAbc + rejectedObs}) are preserved verbatim across every sampled key — no critical placeholder loss observed.
Brand/acronym handling for BehaviorLens, AI, ABA, FBA, BIP, BCBA, BACB, IEP, FERPA, MTSS, RTI, DRO, DTT, GAS, FCT, SCD is consistent — acronyms stay as English.

The AI consent block (consent_title/subtitle/li_* and toggle_title_off/on) and the AI print_disclaimer paragraph are among the most fully-translated sections — these legally-important strings show the translator can produce real Marshallese when prompted, and Latin script with diacritics (ḷ, ņ, ṃ, ọ) is used correctly throughout.
Some idiomatic renderings are strong: cycle_calm = 'Ainem̧m̧an', cycle_peak = 'Eutiejtata', attentionseeking_behavior = 'Ṃwilin in Pukot Kaitokḷọk-Mejān', last_week = 'Wiik eo Ļo̧k'.
risks: **Severe under-translation in tile descriptions (hub.*_desc) and the toast.*/raw.* blocks.** A majority of strings are 70-95% English with only Marshallese function-words (im, ñan, jab, eo, ippan, kwe, ko) substituted. For refugee Marshallese-speaking families who motivated this pack, this is barely better than English-only and may give a false impression of accessibility.

**Three visible MT/string-replacement corruptions ship in user-visible tile titles and tooltips:** cantdowontdo_title = 'maroñ't Do / Won't Do' (apostrophe-t suffix left attached), selfcheck_desc has 'check-ilo' (where 'in'→'ilo' broke the compound 'check-in'), 'Real-iien' compounds spliced into 'Real-time'. These are immediately visible as broken to any reader.

**Lexical mis-glosses and false friends that could harm meaning:** ph.eg_* keys translate 'e.g.' as 'ālkin.' = 'after' (should be a 'for example' marker); biascheck_title 'Lale Ļōmṇak Jab Jim̧we' frames the tool as 'look at unjust thoughts' (accusatory) rather than reflective self-monitoring; condprob_title 'Likely eo elaññe' is grammatically broken; consent uses 'Kōļaļ' (= adjective/quality) where 'consent' should be 'kōṃalim'. A native Marshallese-speaking school-psychology reviewer should re-check the entire hub.* and ai.* namespaces before this pack ships to families.

Inconsistent capitalization (sentence-initial 'in', 'rūkkatak', 'eṃ', 'kajin', 'aolep' lowercased mid-title) suggests an English-source-aware MT pipeline that did not normalize case after substitution.

Reviewer confidence in Marshallese-specific judgments is LOW: Marshallese is a low-resource language for LLMs, and clear failure modes (heavy English residue, broken compounds, mis-glossed function words) were so obvious that even a low-confidence reviewer could flag them; the subtler grammar/agreement judgments still require native review.

strengths: Brand-name preservation is generally correct: BehaviorLens, ABA, BIP, FBA, BCBA, IEP, FERPA, MTSS/RTI, DTT, IOA, GAS, JSON, CSV, AI, Gemini, SMART, TARF, IRP-15 are kept verbatim and not mistranslated.

Placeholders (${var}) appear preserved verbatim across the confirm/system/snapshot strings sampled (remove_counter, delete_goal, remove_contact, delete_self_check, snapshot_too_large, snapshot_rejected_entries, share_code_too_large, contact_removed). No critical placeholder loss observed in the sample.
Sgaw Karen Myanmar script is used consistently for the words that were translated (no mixed Karen/Latin script chaos within the translated tokens themselves).
risks: Pervasive English/Karen code-switching: a native speaker MUST re-translate ABC/AI/hub/cycle/ui strings end-to-end. Roughly 60-80% of mid- and long-form strings sampled contain large amounts of untranslated English content (e.g. 'Real-တၢ်ဆၢကတီၢ်', 'Discrete Trial Training', 'data dashboard', 'consent form', 'cumulative frequency graph'). The pack reads as 'Karenglish' rather than Karen.

Connective particles look wrong/awkward: the translator appears to use 'ဆူ' (lit. 'to/toward') as a literal substitution for English 'to' (infinitive marker), e.g. 'ထၢနုၥ် ABC entries ဆူ ထံၣ်တၢ် progress' — Karen does not form 'to + verb' that way; needs native syntactic review.

Crisis/AI/consent-flow strings (consent_li_*, ai_disabled, ai_turned_on/off, print_disclaimer, consent_ferpa_note) are partly unintelligible and use a coined-looking term 'ဖိၣ်ကိၢ်' for 'student' inconsistently with 'ဖိသၣ်ကၠိ' used elsewhere — risk of meaning loss in the very strings that gate FERPA consent. opdef_desc_v2 contains 'ပှၤသံအတၢ်မၤကွၢ်' which literally reads as 'dead person's review'. These must be re-translated and locally validated before any Karen-speaking family sees them.

strengths: Placeholder preservation is perfect across all 5 sampled placeholder strings (${label}, ${when}, ${count}, ${SNAPSHOT_MAX_ENTRIES.toLocaleString()}) — no template-literal corruption detected.

Brand/acronym discipline is strong: BehaviorLens, AI, ABA, ABC, BIP, DRO, VR, BST, FERPA, GAS, FCT all preserved verbatim while surrounding text is translated; only 17/1427 strings (1.2%) are identical to English and most of those are intentional bare acronyms/proper nouns.

Script is consistently Latin throughout (correct for Falam Chin); morphology shows real translator engagement — productive use of -nak nominalizer, postpositions (ah, in, he), aspectual particles (cangmi, lai, seh) suggesting a human translator rather than MT, with median TR/EN length ratio of 1.00 (no obvious truncation).
risks: Selective skipping of hub.*_desc descriptions: at least ~10 long tile descriptions (hub.biascheck_desc, caseload_desc, cumrecord_desc, dtt_desc, familyvoice_desc, obscoach_desc, predict_desc, scatterplot_desc, socialvalidity_desc, commlog_desc, relmap_desc, snapshot_desc, subtitle) are PURE ENGLISH copies with em-dashes replaced by commas — translator likely batch-skipped a chunk. These are user-visible tile subtitles in the hub, so educators/families will see English in a Falam UI.

Lexical accuracy on abstract psych/SEL vocabulary needs native review — e.g. profile.desc uses 'biaknemhnak' to render the 'personalize' concept; in related Chin languages 'biaknemh-' is the root for worship/reverence, which would be a serious semantic drift. Many ABA/SEL coinages (toidornak for humility, hlettnak for assessment, biaknemhnak for personalization) are translator-invented and need a Falam-speaking school psychologist or SLP to confirm field acceptability.

Long compound paragraphs were not deeply audited for register-appropriate cohesion — the translator's clausal joining (he, le, ti) appears competent but the formal educator-to-family register, honorifics, and culturally-loaded crisis/trauma vocabulary (especially in the crisis, ai.consent, and FERPA-style paragraphs) need a native pilot read before family-facing release.

strengths: Template-literal placeholders (dollar-curly tokens, including complex .toLocaleString() expressions in snapshot_too_large and share_code_too_large) are preserved verbatim across the sampled strings.

Brand/acronym tokens (BehaviorLens, AlloFlow, Gemini, ABA, ABC, IEP, BIP, BCBA, FERPA, DRO, DRA, DRI, DRL, FBA, FCT, IOA, MTSS, RTI, GAS, PCS, AAC, SMART, JSON, CSV, PNG, SVG, PDF, HTML) are consistently kept in their canonical English form.

The newest 'ai.consent_*' block (consent dialog, ai_disabled, ai_turned_off, ai_turned_on) is one of the most thoroughly Hakha-translated areas in the pack — appears to be a hand-pass on safety/FERPA-critical copy where it matters most.
risks: Massive pervasive code-mixing ('Spanglish'): hundreds of strings leave high-frequency English content words (behavior, data, session, schedule, comparison, browser, intervention, checklist, level, trend, pattern, summary, badge, generate, parse, sort, click, tap, generate, modify, document, support, training, ratio, type, persona) untranslated mid-sentence, and routinely leave English function words (during, before, after, from, above, below, when, where, what, who, why, will, will not, every, each, can, could, should, into, across, with, without) in place. A native Hakha Chin reader (especially a refugee parent — the explicit primary audience here) will not be able to read most paragraph-length strings.

Systematic untranslated machine-translation artifact: the English preposition 'to' is repeatedly rendered as the literal token 'ah' (e.g., 'tuah ah follow', 'go ah generate', 'paste ah get', 'Move ah variable schedule', 'ah punish students') — 'ah' is the Hakha locative postposition, not an infinitive marker, so dozens of phrases are grammatically nonsensical. Same for 'asilole' inserted between English clauses as a calque of 'or'.

Lexical inconsistency on the most-repeated user-facing term, 'student' / 'sianginnchung': it appears variously as 'sianginn caa' (most common), 'siangin fa', 'sianginn fa', and 'Sianginn ca' across sibling keys, often with inconsistent capitalization. Also 'no_' is mechanically rendered as 'si lo' producing strings like 'si lo entries yet' / 'si lo data yet' / 'si lo students' that read as broken English with a Hakha clitic glued on, not as Hakha negation. Both patterns need a native speaker pass before release.

Several semantic drift / wrong-word risks a native speaker must verify: 'hub.open_btn' = 'Ka BehaviorLens' ('Ka' = 'My/I', not 'Open'); 'feeling_confident' = 'Lung a tling' (literally 'heart is full/satisfied', not necessarily 'confident'); 'replacement_behavior' rendered both as 'Thlentu Ziaza' (hub.replacementbehavior_title) and 'Hlonhmi tuahsernak' (ph.replacement_behavior...) — same UX concept, two different terms in two adjacent screens.

summary:
## Headline

Across all seven refugee-language behavior_lens packs audited, **none are ship-ready as-is**. The least-bad pack (chin_falam, 72/100) has a localized ~10-tile cluster of pure-English hub descriptions and at least one possibly-religious mistranslation in a privacy string; the rest (lao 52, marshallese 38, acholi 38, maay_maay 28, chin_hakha 28, karen 22) range from "heavy Spanglish" to "substantively English with translated function words." Two packs ship visible character corruption in tile titles (marshallese "maroñ't Do", maay_maay "wuu kara't Do"). One pack (maay_maay) appears to actually be Somali, not Af-Maay. **All seven require native-speaker review before any refugee-family pilot.**

## Per-pack verdict

| Pack | Score | Confirmed defects | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|
| chin_falam_behavior_lens | 72 | 15 | NATIVE-REVIEW (targeted re-translate of ~13 hub_desc keys + `profile.desc`) |
| lao | 52 | 41 | NATIVE-REVIEW after em-dash regex re-run |
| marshallese | 38 | 32 | RE-TRANSLATE hub.*_desc + toast.* + fix 3 corruptions |
| behavior_lens-acholi | 38 | 41 | RE-TRANSLATE hub.*_desc + replace "Matter" calque |
| maay_maay | 28 | 41 | RE-TRANSLATE end-to-end; first confirm language identity (Maay vs Somali) |
| chin_hakha | 28 | 41 | RE-TRANSLATE; fix systemic "ah"-as-infinitive + "si lo"-as-negator |
| karen | 22 | 56 | RE-TRANSLATE; entire pack reads as Karenglish |

## Cross-pack patterns

- **Em-dash preprocessing failure (lao, chin_falam).** Source strings containing `—` were either left fully English with `—`→`,`, or had `—`→`:` with surrounding English retained. ~10-20 hub tile descriptions per pack hit.
- **Function-word-only substitution (acholi, maay_maay, marshallese, karen, chin_hakha).** Translator pipeline replaced only and/with/your/to/this/or, leaving every content noun and verb in English.
- **Calque syntax for English "to" + verb (chin_hakha "ah", karen "ဆူ", marshallese "ñan", maay_maay "ku").** Postpositions repurposed as infinitive markers — grammatically broken in target languages.
- **Visible MT corruption in tile titles (marshallese "maroñ't Do", maay_maay "wuu kara't Do", marshallese "check-ilo", "Real-iien").** Naive string-replace left contraction stubs and broke compounds.
- **Placeholders + acronyms held up everywhere.** Across all 7 packs, `${...}` template literals and BehaviorLens/ABA/FBA/BIP/IEP/FERPA/BCBA preserved. This is the one bright spot.
- **Privacy/FERPA strings degraded in multiple packs** (karen `ai.consent_ferpa_note`, `consent_li_no_real_names`; chin_falam `profile.desc` "biaknemhnak"=worship; marshallese `consent_title` "Kōļaļ"=adjective; chin_hakha `consent_template_exported` left English) — exactly the strings that gate refugee-family data trust.

## Top defects per pack

**lao** — (critical) `behavior_lens.score_1_5` "Score (1,5):" reads as 1.5 decimal in EU notation; (critical) `behavior_lens.ph.share_what_you_notice_at_home_*` Family Voice placeholder is fully English; (major) `behavior_lens.hub.fidelity_title` "Fidelity"→"honesty" (ຄວາມຊື່ສັດ).

**behavior_lens-acholi** — (critical) `educator_hub.educator_hub_behavior_lens_card` ~85% English Tier-1 educator card; (critical) `behavior_lens.hub.hypothesis_desc` leading "A" dropped from "A→B→C", breaking the ABC framework; (major) "Data"→"Matter" calque repeats across dozens of keys.

**maay_maay** — (critical) `behavior_lens.hub.cantdowontdo_title` ships "wuu kara't Do" (corrupted contraction); (critical) pack identity: this is Somali orthography, not Af-Maay; (critical) `behavior_lens.quick_fill_hint` "wuu kara"=he-can used for generic "you can" (gender error).

**marshallese** — (critical) `behavior_lens.hub.cantdowontdo_title` "maroñ't Do" (same contraction bug); (major) `behavior_lens.hub.biascheck_title` reframes bias-reflection as "look at unjust thoughts" (accusatory); (major) `ph.eg_*` keys gloss "e.g."→"ālkin." (after).

**karen** — (critical) `behavior_lens.hub.toggle_title_off` privacy-statement subject garbled ("data leaves your browser" → student-as-subject); (critical) `behavior_lens.ai.consent_ferpa_note` repetitive "နၤနၤပူၤလၢၤနၤ" loop in a FERPA string; (critical) `behavior_lens.hub.hypothesis_desc` leading "A" dropped from "A→B→C".

**chin_falam_behavior_lens** — (major) `profile.desc` uses "biaknemhnak" (likely "worship") for "personalize"; (major) ~10 `hub.*_desc` tile descriptions are pure English with em-dash→comma; (major) `sandbox.complex_desc` mixed-language.

**chin_hakha** — (critical) `behavior_lens.hub.open_btn` "Ka BehaviorLens" = "My BehaviorLens" not "Open"; (critical) `behavior_lens.token.target_placeholder` example may invert the desired behavior; (critical) `behavior_lens.ui.behaviors_can_serve_multiple_functions_*` ~95% English FBA instructional copy.

## Recommended next actions

1. **(Quick-win, P0)** Re-run translation for any source string containing `—`. Fixes ~15-20 critical/major defects in lao + chin_falam in one pass.
2. **(P0)** Confirm maay_maay language identity with a Maine Somali Bantu / Maay community contact before any other Maay work — current pack is Somali.
3. **(P0)** Hand-patch the three corrupted tile titles: marshallese & maay_maay `cantdowontdo_title`, marshallese `selfcheck_desc` "check-ilo", `Real-iien`. These are immediately visible.
4. **(P0)** Build a glossary of ABA terms (consequence, fidelity, reinforcement, caseload, acting-out, replacement behavior, antecedent, data, observation) per pack, then sweep for inconsistencies. Run dev-tools/i18n extractor against descriptive keys (`hub.*_desc`, `ui.*`, `ai.*`, `ph.*`) and re-translate with prompt that explicitly forbids leaving English content words.
5. **(P1)** FERPA/AI/consent block (`ai.consent_*`, `system.ai_turned_*`, `profile.desc`, `toggle_title_*`) gets native-speaker review for every pack — these gate family trust and have already shown semantic drift in karen, marshallese, chin_falam, chin_hakha.
6. **(P1)** Add a CI guard in dev-tools/i18n: flag any string where >40% of tokens after stop-word removal are still ASCII/Latin in a non-Latin-script target pack, and any string containing `'t ` (contraction stub).
7. **(P2)** Cross-pack glossary alignment for the four already-pilot-bound packs (Falam, Hakha) so the same English concept doesn't render two ways across sibling tools (e.g., "mastered", "student", "replacement behavior").

## Honest caveat

This audit was **Claude reviewing Claude** — same model class auditing its own translation work. Self-confidence was reported `low` or `very_low` for every pack. For Marshallese, Sgaw Karen, Falam Chin, Hakha Chin, Acholi, and Maay Maay, training data is sparse and Claude's blind spots are systematic: it can detect obvious English residue and broken compounds (those flagged confidently above), but it cannot reliably judge register, honorifics, dialectal authenticity, culturally-loaded crisis/trauma vocabulary, or whether a coined neologism is field-acceptable to a school psychologist in that language community. Lao is the only pack where confidence in the "lexically-off" calls (Fidelity→honesty, Reinforcement→goods-warehouse, claims→rights) is medium-high because the errors are dictionary-level. **Every pack listed here needs a native-speaker review pass by a member of the AlloFlow pilot family network or a partner Maine refugee organization (Catholic Charities Maine RIS, ProsperityME, Maine Immigrants' Rights Coalition language banks) before being declared shipped to families.**

confirmed.englishSource: — Choose a student —
confirmed.translation: : Choose a student:
confirmed.issue: Em-dash bookends were replaced with colons AND the inner English was never translated. The dropdown placeholder will display literally as ': Choose a student:' to Lao users.
confirmed.suggestion: — ເລືອກນັກຮຽນ —

verdict.reasoning: Verified directly in C:\Users\cabba\OneDrive\Desktop\UDL-Tool-Updated\lang\lao.js line 9619: the value is literally ": Choose a student:" — em-dash bookends replaced with colons AND inner English text never translated to Lao script. Refutation attempts fail: (a) the adjacent key select_student (line 9618) correctly renders "ເລືອກນັກຮຽນ" in Lao script, proving the concept has a known Lao translation and English isn't being preserved as a loanword; (b) the two following keys (pick_adjective, pick_animal) show the same ":English:" corruption pattern, indicating systematic extractor damage rather than a stylistic register choice; (c) Lao uses its own script — Latin English in a dropdown placeholder is not a Lao orthographic convention; (d) the desktop/web-app mirror carries the same broken value, so it will ship to users. Critical untranslated-with-format-corruption defect, suggested fix "— ເລືອກນັກຮຽນ —" is appropriate.

confirmed.englishSource: — Adjective —
confirmed.translation: : Adjective:
confirmed.issue: Same em-dash→colon bug; the dropdown label shows English 'Adjective' verbatim.
confirmed.suggestion: — ຄຳຄຸນນາມ —

verdict.reasoning:
The English source is "— Adjective —" (em-dash framed label, common UI convention for a placeholder option in a dropdown). The Lao translation ": Adjective:" has two clear problems:

1. **Em-dash replaced by colons**: The framing em-dashes (—) have been replaced with colons (:), changing the visual/semantic meaning of the placeholder framing. This is the same systematic em-dash→colon corruption pattern flagged across the pack audit.

2. **"Adjective" left untranslated**: The English word "Adjective" appears verbatim in Latin script. Lao has a well-established native term for adjective — ຄຳຄຸນນາມ (kham khunnaam) — which is the standard grammatical term used in Lao language instruction and dictionaries. "Adjective" is not a loanword in Lao; it would not be written in Latin script in any normal Lao UI or educational context. Lao uses its own script (Lao abugida) and has native grammatical terminology inherited from Pali/Sanskrit traditions.

The suggested fix "— ຄຳຄຸນນາມ —" correctly restores both the em-dash framing and uses the proper Lao grammatical term. This is a real, critical untranslated-English bug.

confirmed.englishSource: — Animal —
confirmed.translation: : Animal:
confirmed.issue: Same em-dash→colon bug; 'Animal' shown in English in the UI.
confirmed.suggestion: — ສັດ —

verdict.reasoning: The English source "— Animal —" is a placeholder/label using em-dashes as decorative bracketing around the word "Animal". The Lao translation ": Animal:" has two clear defects: (1) em-dashes were replaced by colons, which is not a stylistic Lao convention but a mechanical substitution error, and (2) the word "Animal" remains in English rather than being rendered as ສັດ (the standard Lao word for animal). Lao is not a language that borrows "animal" as a loanword — ສັດ is the everyday, unambiguous term. The suggested fix "— ສັດ —" preserves the original em-dash framing and uses the correct Lao lexeme. This matches the same em-dash→colon bug pattern flagged across other packs. Real bug, high confidence.

confirmed.englishSource: Test your ABA knowledge — earn a competency badge at 80%+
confirmed.translation: Test your ABA knowledge, earn a competency badge at 80%+
confirmed.issue: Em-dash replaced with comma but the entire sentence was left in English (no Lao at all). User sees English in the hub tile description.

verdict.reasoning: Verified at C:\Users\cabba\OneDrive\Desktop\UDL-Tool-Updated\lang\lao.js:9683 — the value is literally "Test your ABA knowledge, earn a competency badge at 80%+". Lao uses its own abugida script (ກ ຂ ຄ ງ ...); this string contains only Latin letters, digits, and ASCII punctuation, with zero Lao codepoints. Only difference from English source is em-dash → comma.
